stewart_read: Charles has vented his skepticism and then explicitly grants Meg the floor. This is the functional equivalent of the hello-objection moment — the customer has aired resistance and is now, however grudgingly, inviting a response. The protocol calls for a small ask: acknowledge the framing, ask for 30 seconds to make the case, and if it's not relevant, leave him alone. Meg instead reflected his skepticism back as a closed question ('so it sounds like you're not really looking at solar options for the home. Right?'), which invited him to confirm his own disinterest. Strategic read: Meg may have been trying to clarify before pitching. Reactive read: she pattern-matched to 'not interested' and began her exit sequence one full minute early. The reactive read is better supported — her next substantive move is the exit at 01:52.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg was clarifying Charles's actual intent before investing further. Rejected because her follow-up question ('so it sounds like you're not really looking...') is a leading close, not a genuine probe.
coaching_implication: Show Meg the protocols.hello_objection_not_interested template. The move at 00:40 was to ask for 30 seconds, not to confirm his disinterest. Role-play the moment: 'I totally get that — and you don't know me from anyone. Can I have just 30 seconds to tell you why I'm calling, and if it's not relevant, I'll leave you alone?' That one line keeps the call alive.

quote: I thought that's what I was gonna get, but all I'm gonna get is a sales pitch. I got you.
stewart_read: Charles is not expressing scam anxiety in the traditional door-knocker sense, but he is expressing form-fill regret — he believes the lead-gen mechanism misrepresented what he was signing up for. This is a legitimacy gap, not a product objection. The correct move is honest specificity (per protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number) plus an acknowledgment that his frustration is reasonable, followed by a reframe: 'You're right that the form led you here — and I'm not going to pretend otherwise. But since you're already on the phone, can I ask you one question about what you found in your research?' Meg did not address the form-fill complaint at all.
coaching_implication: Charles's 'I got you' is a trust gap, not a product objection. Coach Meg to separate the two: acknowledge the form-fill frustration explicitly before attempting any pivot. Skipping the acknowledgment means Charles is still carrying the grievance when Meg tries to move forward — which is exactly what happened.

quote: you're gonna pay a 150, $200 a month, which is, you know, what I pay in electricity now
stewart_read: Charles hands Meg his current bill range and frames it as a reason solar doesn't work. This is the objection_inversion entry point: his own number is the leverage. The flip available here is not a fabricated savings claim — it's a reframe of his own math: 'So you're already paying $150-200 a month to the utility. The question isn't whether you pay — you're already paying. The question is whether you keep paying them or start paying yourself.' Meg said 'Mhmm' and let the moment pass entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg was letting Charles finish before responding. Rejected — she never responded to this point at all, even after Charles finished.
coaching_implication: This is the highest-leverage missed moment in the call. Charles did the rep's job for her — he stated his bill, framed the objection, and handed her the flip. Use this clip in the 1-on-1 to demonstrate objection_inversion with his own words. The coaching question: 'What would you have said right here?' If Meg can't answer, that's the training gap. Note: the flip works on his stated number ($150-200) without fabricating any savings figure.

quote: I have a, a slate roof. And so you you can't put you can't put solar panels on a slate roof.
stewart_read: Charles presents the slate roof as a self-DQ. This may be accurate — slate roofs are a legitimate installation challenge — but Charles's information is five years old by his own admission ('it was five years ago'). The probe worth attempting before accepting the DQ is: 'How old is the roof?' A slate roof nearing end-of-life may be a replacement candidate, which opens a different conversation. This is not a clean cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned trigger — Charles expressed no replacement intent — but it is a speculative probe opportunity: explore before accepting the DQ. Meg accepted the DQ without asking.
alternative_hypothesis: The slate roof is a genuine hard DQ and probing wastes both parties' time. Possible — but Charles's five-year-old research basis means the DQ is his assumption, not a verified fact. One question ('how old is the roof?') costs 10 seconds and either confirms the DQ or opens a door.
coaching_implication: Coach Meg on the 'explore before accepting the DQ' discipline from verify.roof_type_age. The question is not 'can we put solar on a slate roof' — it's 'how old is the roof?' If the answer is 15+ years, the conversation shifts. This is not a cross-sell routing moment; it's a probe-before-exit habit. One question, 10 seconds.

quote: I've I've I've done the research myself. Well, I guess it was about five years ago.
stewart_read: Charles's self-identification as an engineer who researches energy technology, combined with the admission that his research is five years old, is an enthusiasm signal hiding inside a skepticism frame. He is not indifferent to solar — he has a formed opinion based on dated information. The verify.interest_in_solar item calls for depth: 'What interested you in solar?' Charles has already answered it unprompted. The entry point is: 'Five years is a long time in this space — what specifically did you look at back then?' That question does two things: it validates his engineer identity and it opens the door to updating his five-year-old data. Meg did not probe this at all.
coaching_implication: Charles's engineer identity and dated research are the most important missed entry point in the call. A rep who validates the research identity ('that's actually helpful — most people haven't looked at this at all') and then asks what specifically he found five years ago is in a real conversation. Coach Meg: the customer's own expertise is not a wall, it's a door. The question 'what did you find?' costs nothing and potentially changes the entire call.

quote: Totally. Well, here, I can just take you off my list here, Charles.
stewart_read: Meg's exit is the call's defining failure — but the exit manner itself is worth separating from the exit timing. The manner is correct: she is gracious, she offers to remove him from the list, she leaves the door open ('if things change, you ever wanna take a look, you've got my number'). That is the right way to exit a call that has genuinely run its course. The problem is the timing: Meg exits after zero probe attempts, zero reframes, and zero use of the objection_inversion opportunity Charles handed her at 01:15. The exit is premature, not rude. The coaching gap is multi_angle_resilience — Meg treated Charles's monologue as a final verdict and skipped the 2-3 probe attempts the schema calls for before conceding.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg correctly read Charles as a hard DQ (slate roof + five-year-old research + bill-equivalence framing) and exited efficiently to protect her time. Possible — but the strategic read requires that Meg attempted at least one probe and found it unworkable. She attempted none. The exit is reactive, not strategic.
coaching_implication: Use this exit as a two-part coaching moment: (1) the manner of exit is a positive model — gracious, non-defensive, door-left-open. Acknowledge that explicitly. (2) The timing is the gap — Meg exited after absorbing a monologue without a single probe. The coaching question: 'At what point in Charles's speech would you have tried to get a word in, and what would you have said?' If Meg can't identify a probe point, that's the training gap on multi_angle_resilience.

quote: Roger that. Thanks for thanks for listening.
stewart_read: Charles's closing line — 'Roger that. Thanks for thanks for listening' — is warmer than his opening. He is not hostile at exit; he is mildly appreciative. This is a small but real signal: the call did not end in antagonism. Meg's gracious exit manner ('Of course. You have a good rest of your day, sir') earned this. The rapport at exit is genuine, which means the relationship was not burned — only the opportunity was missed.
coaching_implication: Show Meg this closing exchange as evidence that her exit manner was right. The lesson is not 'you failed' — it's 'you handled the exit well, and you could have used that same warmth 90 seconds earlier to attempt one probe.' The gap is not personality; it's probe discipline.
